speaker: interviewer
text: Roll three, Maurice Markheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: --doing great. So half were men. And half were women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He build the barracks for so many people and promise his undercover worker and the-- and the-- the-- the-- the-- the [POLISH] used to be the Ukrainians. There was also--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean-- you mean the people who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was watching our--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the guards--

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards, that's right. I'm sorry, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Ukrainian. And we were-- the food we used to get from the Polish kitchen. What so we start to-- you know, we-- sometimes you found a piece-- a piece, you know, pork in the soup. It Wasn't bad. We can't complain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everybody did their best. Because you could keep yourself clean. You got a piece of soap already. You could-- people, we could-- to shave. I mean, we start to look like normal people. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And people had connections with the outside. One fine day, we-- they changed the Plaszow, the arbeitslager Plaszow on that. You know, the one on-- on the-- in the hills there, to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, they changed it into a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's different, a-- a-- a-- a labor camp and a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day and night. The condition, behave. In the concentration camp you could find Jewish, political Aryan, gentile people. You know, all different things, different nationalities. Gypsies, you know, you could find different.

speaker: interviewer
text: An arbeit camp, they was just strictly for work. And here you are not only for work, but you are-- what do you call that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh, I'm sorry. I can't think of that name. [? Mentioned ?] it, I mentioned it, I know. The-- to-- to-- to-- to-- to brainwash--

speaker: interviewer
text: Exhaust you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you first. You know, to take-- take the last-- take the last thing of your soul to the last minute. The difference is also that before I used to belong to the arbeit camp. And When you became concentration camp, you became the property of the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the property of the SS. At that time, when we became property of the SS, the factory had to pay so much and so much money for every worker a day to the SS-- to the SS. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to go-- not-- not to give them nothing. They were taking-- they were charging-- they were supplying us with food, room and board, and otherwise. There you saw the condition. And on the top of those who supply us, there was Goth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? By the way, did they say Goeth? Or did they say Goth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goth, Goth, Goth, Goeth. We call him Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I show his picture of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I got the original picture of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good, I'd love to see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'll show you a picture. I took it. And I-- one day, in Cracow, right after the war, when they call them-- when they escort them to court, I got a picture of him. I spread the picture, already, the copy. But the original picture, I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first thing, what they did, he stopped-- oh we don't have the-- the-- the soup from the Polish kitchens. They were getting-- giving out the Polack soup too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, the Polacks, they didn't have it so green either. But they could survive. They couldn't starve, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were working there. We  were producing. They were satisfied with us. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we-- for the best-- we made the best out of it. We were satisfy-- satisfied too. On the other hand, we find out and we knew that there is Emalia. Emalia was a factory on the other side of Plaszow, [POLISH], also a section, where they used to make pots and pans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was Schindler. You see? And Schindler also took some people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't know if they had Polack or not. He took over the farm-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the factory. When he came to Cracow, I don't want to get involve in that. But I can just tell you only briefly, that [? Bremer ?] deserve also-- but not maybe as-- maybe-- maybe he did more, well, maybe or less. I don't know. But Schindler was copying [? Bremer. ?] How he was copying the [? Bremer. ?] Because they were also going out from the ghetto to the factory, also going out from-- from-- from Plaszow to the factory, like we did. But when we-- but [? Bremer ?] start to build barracks, he start to build barrack. He got the permission to. And eventually, they live on the premises there. But like I said before, he was a-- a-- a-- a top Nazi man, [? Bremer, ?] a pardoner, the real McCoy. He did-- he wasn't bad at all. He didn't kill anybody. But he didn't do what-- what Schindler did. The bottom line is, OK, Schindler was a womanizer. Everybody knew about. Schindler was-- any-- anything, you name it. But the bottom line, he was thinking in advance, maybe I saved those thousand Jews, so I protect myself. Maybe. Maybe, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't-- I-- I-- I'm just presuming only. OK? So for that, he's-- what he got after the war, is coming to him. Bottom line, he saved a thousand Jews. See? That's the way this happens. So here, where was the competition. So they were in competition?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not in competition at all. They knew each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, he was making-- he was making pots and pans. And he was making for the military, for the sub-- for-- for the marines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see? We were making.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go over to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, oh, later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have to jump-- I'll-- I'll tell you why. That's another-- and that's going to be another story. But I have to-- I don't know. I close out with [INAUDIBLE], what we were over there. Eventually, as the Russian-- I don't know, I'm going to make sure. Eventually the Russian approach the Russian front. And before they are at front-- well, they knew. The Germans knew already, they're losing the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all-- they had big, big machinery in that harbor, [INAUDIBLE]. We were-- we were left, all the people, to load it all the-- well, a nail from the wall, we had to take it out and put them on the-- on the railroad. Eventually, we went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: They liquidated. We went up back to Plaszow. At that time, we were lucky. Goth got himself in trouble with the-- with the black market, Goth. Goth. The Gestapo went after him. And they lock him up. He didn't mention it there. I saw him then, later, Goth, too. I came to it. We didn't-- nobody mentioned. I know people don't remember. I don't remember either. Goth wasn't there anymore. What they were doing, there was few places, what people were going in and out to work. But the main work, the main job was Hujowa Gorka.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was there? Over there was digging out the dead bodies and burn them. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know the big mass graves. Because the Russians were approaching. And they didn't want to leave any marks commit here by the German, such as mass graves. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody who came in back from the commando outside went over there to work. And I was working there. The condition up there is unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bodies were smelling. We were smelling. They give us a little water down below. They brought down below because we were digging down, all the-- all the time, dig. I mean, deeper and deeper and deeper. It's-- I-- I can't describe you, with the bare hands, we had to do everything. Sometimes you-- you-- you-- you had a shovel. And you have a-- a-- what-- an axe. Sometime you have the axe, you hit-- you hit somebody in the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you didn't know how the body was laying. So the brains spill all over you. OK? Then there was screaming their selves. Watch at first, discover the face. And when you discovered the face, you stop. And by hand, you had to uncover only the face. What for? What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another-- another halfling, another prisoner with the SS, came down and pulled all the gold teeth. Look over in the mouth. If they have any teeth, they were taken back like that, with gold teeth. Some of them, some people, they had diamonds in their-- in their mouth. You know, they were hiding. They thought maybe they could-- pull all the teeth from the dead. And then later on, just rip it up, all this gold. Sometime you hit it. You-- you hitted an arm. You pulled the arm from the other body.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'll be honest with you, I knew my father is buried there. I knew. I was-- who knows if I didn't dig out, like that, my own father, who got shot in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right? But--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop that? No. No, we can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] What-- what happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] It was such an emotional moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so easy. Who knows if I didn't carry my own father? And throw him? You know, just in the fire. It was a big fire. They have those logs. You know, the railroad logs, what you lay them there, make pyramids, lay down. This one down. Throw it anyplace. Lay it on. You could recognize--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they burned them all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everything. And the clothes. They were in the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had they been buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: At least two years. And I tell you something else, I make myself ahead a little bit. But this, I'm going to mention it specifically. We were-- when we became the property of the SS-- SS, we had to go every week to the main camp, some people, pulling a horse bug-- a buggy with-- with rations for bread. Because we are getting, still, the three ounces bread. You know, the things what, they're making the soup, to the main camp. One day, my brother-- let him rest in peace-- he arranged it. I want to-- we want to see each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through somebody, you know somebody. I was the horse. You know? The buggy, you pull by horse, you know? You know? You were the horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the horse. I was glad to do it, one day, one week. As we approached the camp, there's a [? barrier, ?] you know, the-- the-- the-- you know, the ramp to open up and close. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS or sometimes Lithuanians, or the Ukrainians, they were watching us. This was the guy-- no. I forgot, before you helped me, the [POLISH], well, they were watching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were-- no, no, no. They were Ukrainians. They were Lithuanians. And they were Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see? They were watching us. All of a sudden, they don't want to let us into the camp. Well, you have to-- you approach the-- the-- the entry, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: As I pull, they stop us. Why can't you-- what-- why'd they stop us? The Ukrainian came out and he said, look, at-- a-- a-- a couple trucks has to go by first. And Goth going to be-- Goth going to be here any minute. I said, already, uh-huh. Is that the end of us? What for I went to see-- what for I want to see my brother? What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm thought, God, Goth going to see that, going to ask what we do here. So he's going to kill us. But now, they push us on the side, said, don't worry. You're going to get in. You're going to get the-- you know, the men-- the-- the-- the food for the whole camp. And Goth, is walking, come over. And he's walking back and forth already, back and forth already. Didn't look at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened maybe 15 minutes later? Two full trucks of Polacks, gentile people. All bound up against the tailgate from the trucks-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --pull over.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what's going to happen with this. So I remember, and I see him, his face today, I would recognize him. And he start to-- they to sing, you know, the national hymn, the Polish National Hymn. Because they knew they-- they-- they-- where they'd carried them.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't know. Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] And I'm going to say it in Polish. And then translate it. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's what they sang?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Idiots--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polacks--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're not helping the Jews now. But can't you see what's happened? They start with the Jews but they end with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prisoner, the Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: --prisoner on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Said that. You see? And there was two carloads of them. I don't know how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they went to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: And five-- yeah, they went-- no, no, no. They went to Hujowa Gorka. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ramp open up. They count them. The ramped open up. And the truck went up to the hill there. They un-- un-- un-- unbundled them, free. And ask-- they-- they told them to jump. As they were jumping, you got-- you got-- you got-- you got a-- a-- a bullet. As he was calling, boom, boom, boom. We could hear, downstairs. Because this was not too far away, on the hill. And we could hear, downstairs, every bullet-- --as they were jumping to the hole, you know, to the ravine, there was-- there was a big-- to the mass graves there. That was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea why they did that to Polacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they caught them. They brought them-- they brought him from-- who knows? We didn't know for what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were free men-- they were free people. They caught them in the street. Maybe they were suspicious to them. Maybe they was the underground. Maybe they were making black market. We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we know? What we know? We didn't think, even, about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was Polacks. Oh, there were Polacks there too, in our camp. There were some-- then later on, I worked with the Polack in the-- in the garage too, over there. That Polack came from Auschwitz to work here in the garage. You know, so they were-- they were-- they-- they got their share too. No, nothing to compare. But they were-- they were allowed to have a-- you know, packages from home. You know, like bread and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were prisoners. You see? Some of them, they wore triangle. Yellow, red, or green. Every-- every color represent a different things what he is, you know? Communist, red, communist. Green, political. you know? This is-- Homosexual?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. I wouldn't even think of that. There is-- there is such a thing. Homosexual, well. Well, anyway-- oh, and this was before. Then later, I went and I work there. When we came from [? cabins, ?] they liquidate [INAUDIBLE]. They went all the way. So they sent us up to dig the dead bodies. And one day, we're coming down. I was lucky I was working at night. So the smell wasn't so bad, like daytime. You know, when the sun burns on you. And they don't let us into the barracks. And I hear, already, that they make up a Schindler list, here-- here, when I came to [? hand ?] [? over. ?] Because it's still quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler List? Schindler? Who's Schindler? Emalia, you know, where they make pots and pans. But then he changed to make a-- a-- for something else, for the-- for the-- for the Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody wants to go on that list. So first will go the people who are employed by him. They-- they live there, in the factory, first. But then was a man named Marcel Goldberg. Just a Jew like me. Just also from Cracow, the monster. You see, he were-- a whole-- a whole-- a whole thing. For money, you could get in on the list. Because he was making the list. He was bumping up people, what they worked there, for Schindler. Schindler got mad later, at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he didn't know. You think he-- he-- he was interested in every individual? No. No way.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not so far. Of course not. The thing-- the thing that-- I-- I-- I don't want to mention it. It don't want to say anything about the Page.

speaker: interviewer
text: The way he write it, Page? No, it's not too awkward. But I tell you the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: In some respects, I give him respect. Because when he wrote the book-- when the guy from Australia wrote the book, he call me, beg me that he wants to interview. He wants to talk to me. And believe you me, I couldn't make myself available. I had, at that time, a family. I couldn't afford to lose-- to lose work. I had to work. I couldn't-- I couldn't afford to lose the day's work. I couldn't. I was [? learning ?] myself. But anyway-- and people, they're calling the people from the list. The list is out already. And people, for money, they're running back and forth. You know, they're making deals. It's the same thing. It's not what you know, whom you know. So it's-- there were people between ages-- between like, [? and agency, ?] maybe Between them, then they got in to him, to Marcel Goldberg. He take-- he-- he took the bribery. I had no money. I had no money. And all of a sudden, I hear they call my name. They're loading, already, the people to the boxcar. They're calling my name. So I didn't go anymore to the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't know, God must have-- now I come back to God. Maybe God was over me. And they call me. And I went-- and that's the way I went into Schindler. You see? And-- How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I tell you the truth, I was already hungry, cold, all broke down. I didn't think. But I knew, it's-- it's a hope. If you go to Schindler, it's a hope to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I knew. It's a hope only. What--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, 18? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, by Schindler, we-- we knew we're going to survive. But also, you could only-- to feed 1,000 people is not so simple. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not so simple. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in other words, if we had additional-- they didn't-- the SS didn't give us anything. But if Schindler give something, he could give you a little bit only. You were still hungry. You were still after the potato, or whatever you could find. Whatever, what, anything. We were still hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you assured one thing. You won't die from bullet, from getting a bullet in your head. You see? If you die, you would die from starvation.

speaker: interviewee
text: But actually, over there, you couldn't starve either. But I don't know, if this would go on longer, probably we would. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One fine day-- I have to skip. One fine day is-- is a rumor that Goth, they let him out from the prison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he's going to walk by the factory with his girlfriend. But he said, Schindler, I'm going to be with him. And remember one thing. He has no authority or any power. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was just let out. So when he was let out, so he came to Brinnlitz, to Schindler. Probably, you know, because they found out he comes from Vienna. They found, in his apartment, all the way to the-- to the ceiling, liquor and-- and-- and cigarettes. All the way, that's how much he had. A-- a-- a fortune.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, that's why they investigate him. From where you got that? You had no bribe. You know, you're suppose to SS, belongs to the government. You see? That's the way he got-- and he got off. And I think he went on once and he testified for him too.

speaker: interviewee
text: What, from Schindler, from the Plaszow, we went to Gross Rosen. And we stayed there a few days. And then to Brinnlitz, another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, don't worry about it. He has no authority. He has no power.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, I don't think so. I-- I'm not sure that he said, even he doesn't carry a gun in the morning. But they let him out for a few days or something. He went by. Believe you me, before he was husky, big. He came out skinny, like that. Like you see him on the-- you're going to see him in the picture. He wasn't the same, Goth. He probably got, you know, a good investigation.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have given you satisfaction, to see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, yes, yes. But everybody you know, has to say-- take off their hat when he walk by. You know, give him their respect. Because you're afraid. But to certain people, he said, no power. He has no authority. Don't worry about anything. You're not going to do anything here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a personal relationship with Schindler at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I saw him. I was close to him. Sometimes you know, he walked by like I-- like I'm sitting next to you here. But he'd walk by. He ask how you doing? I said, fine. Never complained. I had a closest-- a [? co-accident ?] with Mrs. Schindler once. She-- there came once a big truck with bread, full of bread. And they called me to unload the bread to the warehouse, from the truck to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was carry like that. And we are-- naturally, we were supervised by the SS. The SS was watching us. They were watching that nobody steals it. She blink with her eyes on me. And I understood that, to steal a bread. You know what a whole bread means? She what? She--

speaker: interviewee
text: She blinked me with her eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, blinked. I see, steal the bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like that. And she was talking to the-- to the SS man who was watching us. In other words, she makes him busy with a conversation or something. And I took the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? That-- that happen with her. That's the only one-- one time I-- I had [? talked ?] [? honestly, ?] with her. I try-- when she was here last year, I try to remind her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't remember. She couldn't remember. Which I don't blame her. And then I saw  him here. But you know, he was saying that he remembers everybody. But no, everybody remembers him. He couldn't. I mean, let's don't fool our self. He couldn't remember everybody. And then I remember the way he said goodbye to us. That night, I remember they call a roll call, it was. And he had a little speech. And we could  hear, already, the-- the Russian Front approach closer, closer. You could see on the road, because this camp was a little bit-- on like, on a-- you know, like, in a canyon. The SS army was running away. So he came and he says, number one, he said to us-- this what I know of it, as an eyewitness.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, tomorrow-- what he said to the-- to the Germans, I don't know. I'm presuming only, this what they said in the pictures. You know how they said, in the pictures? But what actually happens, he came, he start to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, remember one thing. When you get up tomorrow morning-- this was at night, maybe about 10 o'clock at night. It was dark. You will find there'll be no guard. The whole guard will disappear. Sit tight. Don't move. Don't go nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there is the Ukrainian Army. They're very antisemitic. What is his name? The General? Oh, I forgot his name. Wolski? I don't remember that name.

speaker: interviewee
text: From General-- I mean, from Ukrainian Army, the whole army, and the rest [INAUDIBLE], if they will find out that there is some Jews here, they're going to drive in. And they're gonna kill everybody. And the grave is prepared there for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't move at all. We sit there like that for a couple more days. A couple people start to run once. They thought that's the Russian Army because we were in the canyon there. So they're mistaken. You know, maybe we didn't-- they didn't see good. And this was still German escaping. So they opened fire on them. They're lucky they only wounded them. And then there was quiet. Oh, he supply us with certain things. He gave us a-- a-- a-- no. Material. No, what do you call it? The clothes for shoes, you know? For-- for underwear, for a shirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: He prepare that, Schindler, for all of us. Oh  yeah, that's the first pen I had. When I want to Poland, I sold that on the black market. It was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: A pen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, But they don't mention it, those things. That's the first money I had, the Polish money after liberation, from that. You see, everybody. And then a few days-- a couple more days. And then far, far, far away, we see a-- a-- something that's-- it's not a horse. It's not a cow. It's a donkey. And there is sitting a Russian officer, you know, with all the medals. And a donkey, driving a donkey in our direction. As he approached-- [LAUGHS] --we saw this is a Russian soldier. He was-- so he was an officer. I don't know what kind, with all the medals, on a donkey. So nobody spoke the Russian. But some people spoke, already, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they want to talk to him. And he said, yes, I'm a Jew. So we ask him where he comes from, that he comes?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yeah, I'm going on a donkey, you know, with gun, with everything. All by himself. He said, don't worry. In the next few hours, the whole army's going to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ask him what he pass by. So he pass by already, those camps and everything. They know-- they knew already the massacre, the killing. Auschwitz, he said, there's nothing left there from Auschwitz. They-- they demolish everything, you know what I mean is? That's what you see it today. And that-- that way I went back to Cracow. Why I went to Cracow? I went to my home, where I was born, where I was raised. And I went-- I went to our-- to our-- you know, the-- the janitor. I work on that here. I remind myself, my own apartment. You know why? Because before my mom went to ghetto, she took out all her furnitures and took other furnitures and put it in her apartment. We give it to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody need the furniture at that time. There were 10-- 10 families in one room. 10 people in one room, laying in the ghettos. And he says, [? well, man you should-- ?] yeah, don't worry about anything. I have some money. You don't have to worry. You stay with me. You live with me. I give you-- whatever you want, whatever you need, I'm going to give it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked about the son. He said, my son was-- they-- they shoot-- they-- they send him to Auschwitz. And you know what they were doing in Auschwitz. Never heard from him.
